Hi, and welcome back to Line Sheet. The gang\xe2\x80\x99s all here\xe2\x80\x94Rachel Strugatz, Sarah Shapiro, and yours truly\xe2\x80\x94for this very special mailbag issue, where we address everything from the Blake Lively haircare sitch to the looming threat of tariffs on the fashion industry. You asked so many fabulous questions that I may have to run a Part 2 next week. As a bonus, up top Sarah and I get into the implications of the Saks-Neiman Marcus Group deal, which finally, finally, finally closed this past Monday.

Dec 26, 2024

Greetings! As you can probably tell, today\xe2\x80\x99s newsletter looks a little different. After three years, we\xe2\x80\x99ve redesigned the look and feel of our private emails, and want to know what you think. If you have any feedback, please click here to take a brief survey.

Lauren Sherman

Hi, and welcome back to Line Sheet. Happy Boxing Day.

The gang\xe2\x80\x99s all here\xe2\x80\x94 Rachel Strugatz , Sarah Shapiro , and yours truly\xe2\x80\x94for this very special mailbag issue, where we address everything from the Blake Lively haircare sitch to the looming threat of tariffs on the fashion industry. You asked so many fabulous questions that I may have to run a Part 2 next week. As a bonus, up top Sarah and I get into the implications of the Saks-Neiman Marcus Group deal , which finally, finally, finally closed this past Monday.

\xf0\x9f\x9a\xa8\xf0\x9f\x9a\xa8 Programming note : Tomorrow on Fashion People , I\xe2\x80\x99m joined by Sunita Kumar Nair , author of CBK: Carolyn Bessette Kennedy: A Life in Fashion . Yes, we talk about what you think we talk about (the internet\xe2\x80\x99s obsession with Bessette-Kennedy), but Sunita also shares how she became the one to write this book, which you may find equally fascinating. Listen here and here .

\xf0\x9f\x9b\x8d\xef\xb8\x8f For those of you with the shoppies : This is a great time to get something for yourself, especially if you are hard to satisfy in the gifting department. In between building Lego sets, I\xe2\x80\x99ve spent my off time this week scouring The RealReal and Vestiaire Collective for Calvin Klein 205W39NYC blazers, Chanel dresses (there are so many , proof that the ready-to-wear business is legit) and blue velvet everything. (Check out this Ala\xc3\xafa dress made for Tina Turner .) Also, a P.S.A.: If you like shopping curated vintage, don\xe2\x80\x99t sleep on Resee , an operation run out of a showroom on Avenue Montaigne by an American. Everything is special, and prices are competitive.

On the subject of Ala\xc3\xafa, I also recommend searching the multibrand sites for fresh sales. They buy Ala\xc3\xafa deep and there are real deals at Saks Fifth Avenue (arguably the best selection), Nordstrom , and Bergdorf Goodman . Of course, the dress I want is not on sale. (Actually, there are two .)

Finally, be sure to poke around La Gar\xc3\xa7onne where you\xe2\x80\x99ll find Junya , Auralee , Plan C , and more. There isn\xe2\x80\x99t a better mix online.

Mentioned in this issue : Marc Metrick , Tracy Margolies , Geoffroy van Raemdonck , Authentic Brands Group, G-III, Bloomie\xe2\x80\x99s and Bloomingdale\xe2\x80\x99s, Bernard Arnault , Willi Smith, Geoffrey Beene, Bill Blass, L\xe2\x80\x99Etiquette, Blake Lively , Danielle Steel , Lucky magazine, Charlotte Collet , Sarah de Mavaleix , Emily Weiss , Glossier, and many more\xe2\x80\xa6

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

Don\xe2\x80\x99t drop the ball. New Year\xe2\x80\x99s Eve is just around the corner\xe2\x80\x94and you deserve your main character moment. Book your glam for the big night!

It\xe2\x80\x99s a Richard Baker Christmas Miracle! The Saks-Neiman Deal Finally Closed

On Monday at about 5:30 p.m. ET, after months\xe2\x80\x94 years , really\xe2\x80\x94of speculation and expectation, Saks Global and the Neiman Marcus Group notified employees that the former\xe2\x80\x99s acquisition of the latter was complete. On the H.R. front, it\xe2\x80\x99s no surprise that Neiman Marcus Group C.E.O. Geoffroy van Raemdonck is exiting the group, which will be run by Saks Global C.E.O. Marc Metrick . The big promotion you\xe2\x80\x99ll care about is Tracy Margolies , who was named president of Bergdorf Goodman, the portfolio\xe2\x80\x99s true jewel. Tracy has volleyed between Saks and Bergdorf for her entire career, and this must feel good. It\xe2\x80\x99s also worth interrogating how the transaction was financed: New investors include Amazon (let\xe2\x80\x99s see how that manifests itself), Authentic Brands Group (which owns the license for Barneys New York), Salesforce (they have a data and A.I. partnership for customer service), M. Klein & Company, G-III Apparel Group (classic Seventh Avenue stuff), plus the $2.2 billion in senior debt, plus a revolving credit facility. (Also, all of Saks Global\xe2\x80\x99s existing investors are staying in.)

Anyway, there\xe2\x80\x99s lots to unpack here, as they say. To start, here\xe2\x80\x99s a little bit of what Sarah and I discussed in the hours and days after the deal was announced:

  • Lauren : As someone who has worked at department stores, and with department stores, what do you think the Saks-Neiman merger says about the future of multibrand retail? On Monday, the Nordstrom family also announced plans to take their company private. Remember, we also had the (yet-to-close) YNAP-Mytheresa deal this year. And the Farfetch deal. Wow, that\xe2\x80\x99s a lot! Five years ago, the future of these businesses looked very different.

    Sarah : It\xe2\x80\x99s challenging! Pretty much every brand sells direct-to-consumer, now, too, which has increased competition and costs to acquire customers online. Then there\xe2\x80\x99s inventory management, which feels like it\xe2\x80\x99s harder than ever, and shrinking profits, thanks to all of the above and rampant discounting. Unsurprisingly, we are seeing multibrand retailers consolidate and merge\xe2\x80\x94or go private away from prying eyes\xe2\x80\x94to help acquire customers, connect to more brands, and to boost shareholder value through real estate expansion. This is the only way to compete against the headwinds.

    Meanwhile, retailers will continue to \xe2\x80\x9ctest-learn-scale\xe2\x80\x9d to grow. Nordstrom local, the smaller format Bloomie\xe2\x80\x99s stores, and the Saks Media Network are all examples of how retailers are trying to stay relevant and grow, or at least slow the downfall. The real question is whether these multibrand retailers can use this opportunity to innovate and reinvigorate the state of shopping. How do you think luxury brands feel about this acquisition? Does it change anything for them?

    Lauren : Well, I am sure some of them are a little worried, even though Saks Global has made it clear that the Neiman Marcus brand isn\xe2\x80\x99t going anywhere. The brands have had a lot of leverage over the department stores for the past few years: they needed the brands, which have their own stores, more than the brands needed them. Now that it\xe2\x80\x99s all one big company\xe2\x80\x94and luxury spending has slowed\xe2\x80\x94this new combined entity has more negotiating power. Do you think it\xe2\x80\x99ll change anything for the customer? One reader asked if we thought the end-of-season sales won\xe2\x80\x99t be as good.

    Sarah : There are still plenty of stores not owned by Saks Global that sell most of these brands, so you\xe2\x80\x99ll continue to see competitive prices. Sales have more to do with inventory management, anyway, and that\xe2\x80\x99s always a gamble. Don\xe2\x80\x99t worry, you\xe2\x80\x99ll still be able to score.

    Lauren : Of course, that\xe2\x80\x99s one of the reasons this deal went through with flying colors while the Tapestry-Capri deal got blocked . Saks and Neiman Marcus sell pure luxury goods\xe2\x80\x94the F.T.C. doesn\xe2\x80\x99t usually care about making sure rich people get a deal. The sticking point with Tapestry-Capri wasn\xe2\x80\x99t necessarily full-priced goods, but the potential of reduced competition between Coach and Michael Kors at the outlet stores. That\xe2\x80\x99s not an issue here.

    Anyway, there\xe2\x80\x99s a lot riding on this merger. Sarah, what do Saks Global chairman Richard Baker , as well as Marc and the team, need to do to make sure this is a success?

    Sarah : If I were them, I would close a bunch of stores and rent those spaces to others, reduce the footprint of many existing stores, and ensure the selection in each remaining store is at once practical (what do people actually need to buy in-store these days) and inspiring (there must also be a discovery element). It is really, really hard to do that, but you have to give people a reason to walk in.

And now, your burning questions\xe2\x80\xa6

Blake Beautyology, LVMHUSA Fantasies & More

A pan-Line Sheet mailbag with answers to your burning questions about the entire fashion-beauty-retail industrial complex.

Lauren Sherman
Rachel Strugatz
Sarah Shapiro

There\xe2\x80\x99s something for everyone in this end-of-year mailbag, whether you\xe2\x80\x99re a strategic-group obsessive (yes, they exist), a Blake Lively hater or stan (yes, they, too, exist), or simply betting on the date of the next Chanel price hike (we do not endorse gambling). The breadth of the questions here reflects the influence of the fashion and beauty industries on the culture at large, so we\xe2\x80\x99ve assembled a dream team to answer them: beauty correspondent Rachel Strugatz , retail expert Sarah Shapiro , and of course, yours truly. We\xe2\x80\x99ll be covering it all as Line Sheet goes to five days a week starting January 6. Let\xe2\x80\x99s get started\xe2\x80\xa6

Why is an American LVMH so difficult to achieve, besides the heritage of the brands and the fact that Arnault saw this all before everyone else did and his timing cannot be duplicated?

Lauren : I\xe2\x80\x99m pretty sure I\xe2\x80\x99ve answered this question before, but it\xe2\x80\x99s a perennial one, so I\xe2\x80\x99m happy to revisit it. The short answer is that the U.S. does not have the infrastructure to build pure luxury brands\xe2\x80\x94the manufacturing capabilities, the talent pool, or the history. A proprietor could scoop up a bunch of well-known, all-but-dead American brands\xe2\x80\x94say Geoffrey Beene, Bill Blass, and Willi Smith\xe2\x80\x94but even if they attached cool designers and great marketers to each, there is no way to easily scale that without leather goods capabilities, and even then it\xe2\x80\x99s increasingly difficult because of all the competition. When Bernard Arnault hired Marc Jacobs to design Louis Vuitton in the late \xe2\x80\x9990s, LV was already a pretty big brand. Who knows if it would work now.


Look at The Row, which got backing from the Wertheimers and others this past year. The Row could only grow to a certain point in the U.S. before it had to move a meaningful amount of its operations to Paris. As I\xe2\x80\x99ve always said, brands and operators should not focus on replicating something that already exists\xe2\x80\x94in fashion, and most other businesses, that strategy almost never works. Also, LVMH has changed, too. Arnault\xe2\x80\x99s mission is no longer about resurrecting dead brands, but rather restructuring existing ones.

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

Don\xe2\x80\x99t drop the ball. New Year\xe2\x80\x99s Eve is just around the corner\xe2\x80\x94and you deserve your main character moment. Book your glam for the big night!

What\xe2\x80\x99s your take on this whole Blake Lively sitch?

Rachel : This one is complicated. Blake Brown Beauty had very strong sales at launch, which is often the case with celebrity brands, and especially ones with wide retail distribution from the jump. I initially reported that retail sales for Lively\xe2\x80\x99s haircare line at Target reached close to $5 million in the first three and a half weeks\xe2\x80\x93\xe2\x80\x93and then took a serious hit. I viewed data from two sources that showed sales dropped by more than 87 percent between August 11, their peak, and September 15, which correlates with Justin Baldoni \xe2\x80\x99s alleged smear campaign.

It hasn\xe2\x80\x99t gotten much better since. According to Target sales data I viewed, Blake Brown averaged about $191,000 per SKU per week from its July 31 launch through Nov 3. But during the four-week period ending November 3, the brand averaged about $93,000 per SKU per week\xe2\x80\x94an enormous decline. A 30 percent drop-off is normal, but a 50 percent drop is something . The dip in sales and change in sentiment regarding Lively and the brand was unusually extreme. In what felt like days, the narrative around Blake Brown went from an early success story to people saying they were returning her products. I do wonder if sales will rebound a bit in light of the lawsuit and a number of public figures coming to Lively\xe2\x80\x99s defense. A person with knowledge of Target\xe2\x80\x99s beauty business said a \xe2\x80\x9chair reset\xe2\x80\x9d at the retailer is imminent, and while probably unrelated, Blakely\xe2\x80\x99s lawsuit is conveniently timed. But ultimately, this person reminded me, beauty brands live and die by their product quality, value, etcetera, so while Lively\xe2\x80\x99s personal drama may be dominating headlines yet again, it\xe2\x80\x99s the ratings, reviews, and repeat purchases that determine long-term success.

What\xe2\x80\x99s going to happen with price inflation at brands next year? Plain black ballerina flats can\xe2\x80\x99t possibly get much more expensive!

Sarah : Well, everything can always get more expensive, but aggressive price increases may finally be cooling off. Consumer packaged goods companies pulled back on price hikes in 2024, and fashion retail appears to be following suit as customers show clear resistance to paying more. This shift in consumer behavior shows that perhaps brands have less control in the pricing dynamics at the moment. The luxury resale market continues to grow year over year, giving customers an alternative to owning luxury at sharper price points. Chanel only increased their handbag prices once in 2024. In previous years, the company had traditionally hiked prices twice, typically in March and September.

Globally, brands are diversifying sourcing and production to help combat inflation and cost increases. Looking ahead to 2025, we\xe2\x80\x99re likely to see more emphasis on accessible price points and value-focused offerings, like Uniqlo\xe2\x80\x99s sub $100 cashmere. (I\xe2\x80\x99m also excited to explore the Quince of it all in future issues of Line Sheet.) As for whether the impending, increasingly probable tariffs will affect fashion? You won\xe2\x80\x99t see that impact for at least a year or two, depending on if and how they are enacted.

Can you please please, please share your current fashion inspiration sources? Online, print, whatever\xe2\x80\xa6 I subscribe to L\xe2\x80\x99Etiquette (love, thank you!), but I\xe2\x80\x99m Substack\xe2\x80\x99d out (so bored) and influencers on TikTok feel fake. I miss Lucky magazine, ugh.

Lauren : Oh man, it\xe2\x80\x99s tough! I only go on TikTok for work. L\xe2\x80\x99Etiquette is probably my No. 1 source at the moment. I still use Instagram a lot, and follow people whose style I love. ( Maryam Nassirzadeh , Daphne Javitch , and Danielle Steel are some favorites.) The best thing is to follow stylists whose work you admire. I\xe2\x80\x99d say that Charlotte Collet (who works with Bally, Auralee, and styles a ton of editorials) has been the most influential on me over the past year, but Sarah de Mavaleix is a constant. (If you have received my Paris recommendation list over the years, most of it came from Sarah.)

Stores that shoot the clothes really well\xe2\x80\x94like Desert Vintage \xe2\x80\x94also get me going. I still pick up magazines for inspiration, though that behavior is usually driven by a stylist tipping me off by touting an editorial they worked on. One extremely fun place to look is Japanese fashion magazines\xe2\x80\x94the O.G. inspiration for Lucky . I like Fudge (very preppy and youthful), but Popeye is the big one. They mostly feature men\xe2\x80\x99s clothing but\xe2\x80\xa6 same diff. The best place to be inspired, though, is the street. Even if you don\xe2\x80\x99t go to Paris on the regular, you can look up street style imagery from Paris on the regular.

Do you think Glossier will finally be acquired in 2025?

Rachel : Ah, this was my second -most-asked beauty question over the course of the year, behind only who the next C.E.O. of Est\xc3\xa9e Lauder will be. ( St\xc3\xa9phane de La Faverie officially starts in his new role in six days, by the way.) For the better part of a decade, a Glossier sale has been one of the most discussed potential acquisitions in the beauty industry, but interest in the brand\xe2\x80\x99s future has definitely peaked the last two-ish years, following some highly publicized \xe2\x80\x9cgrowing pains,\xe2\x80\x9d founder Emily Weiss \xe2\x80\x99s decision to step down as C.E.O., a Sephora rollout, a pretty successful \xe2\x80\x9cturnaround,\xe2\x80\x9d and, most importantly, the engagement of Morgan Stanley to explore sale options.

Sure, an acquisition is always possible , but Glossier unfortunately came up during a time of tech-level valuations, cartoonish fundraises, and prioritizing top-line growth over profitability. Until the brand becomes profitable for real (or can show a viable path to meaningful profitability), it\xe2\x80\x99s highly unlikely it will sell for anywhere close to $1.8 billion\xe2\x80\x94its valuation at its last round, in 2021. I heard that Glossier\xe2\x80\x99s EBITDA for this year is expected to be in the mid-single-digit millions, and about twice that in 2025. If you do that math, even a best-case scenario\xe2\x80\x93\xe2\x80\x93a 20x multiple on, say, $15 million EBITDA\xe2\x80\x93\xe2\x80\x93would only amount to about a $300 million sale.

Of course, to simply talk of EBITDA and multiples would be oversimplifying an extremely nuanced situation and discount the company\xe2\x80\x99s revenue, which would be optimized by a strategic (more distribution, lower costs, eliminated redundancies, etcetera). Glossier literally invented the millennial beauty playbook, is strong in multiple categories (makeup, skin, and fragrance), and has proven it can overcome real challenges (evolving from its D.T.C.-only roots, transitioning from a millennial to Gen Z brand, etcetera). I know that the two fragrance launches in September were very successful, but we still don\xe2\x80\x99t yet know what re-purchase rates will look like or how they\xe2\x80\x99ll ultimately impact profitability in 2025.

Sarah, you spent a good chunk of your career as a buyer at Bloomingdale\xe2\x80\x99s. Is there even a path to becoming a store buyer-slash-merchant these days?

Sarah: Buying training programs still exist, but they\xe2\x80\x99ve evolved. Traditional retail giants have rebranded them as \xe2\x80\x9cleadership development programs,\xe2\x80\x9d where you\xe2\x80\x99ll rotate through different roles before landing as an assistant buyer and eventually a buyer running a department.


Here\xe2\x80\x99s what I think has really changed: Many aspiring retail execs are learning the ropes at direct-to-consumer brands and retail startups. I\xe2\x80\x99m sure you\xe2\x80\x99ve heard the popular startup idiom about \xe2\x80\x9cbuilding the plane while you fly it.\xe2\x80\x9d If the startup succeeds ( big if! ), you could ride the wave of career growth as it expands. Whether you choose the structured or startup route, success still boils down to two things: mastering P&L management and having that sixth sense for what customers want. Those skills are never going away.

That\xe2\x80\x99s it from Line Sheet this week. R.I.P. bag charms.

Until Monday,

Lauren

An insider-friendly tip sheet from Matthew Belloni, who spent 14 years in the trenches at The Hollywood Reporter and five before that as an entertainment lawyer. Subscribers also receive What I\xe2\x80\x99m Hearing+, a companion email focused on entertainment law, the streaming industry, and more.
